Dendrimer-based drug delivery systems: history, challenges, and latest developments
===================================================================================

*   [Juan Wang](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#auth-Juan-Wang-Aff1)[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Aff1), 
*   [Boxuan Li](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#auth-Boxuan-Li-Aff2)[2](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Aff2), 
*   [Li Qiu](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#auth-Li-Qiu-Aff1)[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Aff1), 
*   [Xin Qiao](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#auth-Xin-Qiao-Aff1)[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Aff1)&
*   …
*   [Hu Yang](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#auth-Hu-Yang-Aff3)[3](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Aff3)

Show authors
[_Journal of Biological Engineering_](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/)**volume 16**, Article number:18 (2022) [Cite this article](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#citeas)

*   [Metrics details](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Since the first dendrimer was reported in 1978 by Fritz Vögtle, dendrimer research has grown exponentially, from synthesis to application in the past four decades. The distinct structure characteristics of dendrimers include nanoscopic size, multi-functionalized surface, high branching, cavernous interior, and so on, making dendrimers themselves ideal drug delivery vehicles. This mini review article provides a brief overview of dendrimer’s history and properties and the latest developments of dendrimers as drug delivery systems. This review focuses on the latest progress in the applications of dendrimers as drug and gene carriers, including 1) active drug release strategies to dissociate drug/gene from dendrimer in response to stimuli; 2) size-adaptive and charge reversal dendrimer delivery systems that can better take advantage of the size and surface properties of dendrimer; 3) bulk and micro/nano dendrimer gel delivery systems. The recent advances in dendrimer formulations may lead to the generation of new drug and gene products and enable the development of novel combination therapies.

Introduction
------------

### Dendrimer: a brief history and its unique properties

The word “dendrimer” derives from a Greek phrase of “dendron”, which means tree or meros or branch [[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR1 "Vögtle F, Richardt G, Werner N. Introduction. In: Vögtle F, Richardt G, Werner N, editors. Dendrimer chemistry: concepts, syntheses, properties, applications. Wiley: Academic; 2009. p. 1–24.")]. As early as 1978, Buhleir and coworkers synthesized and reported the first “cascade” and “nonskid-chain-like” molecules with molecular cavity topologies, which later were recognized as the early forms of dendritic polymers [[2](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR2 "Buhleier E, Wehner W, Vögtle F. “Cascade”- and “nonskid-chain-like” syntheses of molecular cavity topologies. Synthesis. 1978;9:155–8.")]. From 1979 to 1985, Donald A. Tomalia and his coworkers at the Dow Laboratories made a breakthrough in the development of dendrimers [[3](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR3 "Tomalia DA, Baker H, Dewald J, Hall M, Kallos G, Martin S, et al. A new class of polymers: starburst-dendritic macromolecules. Polym J. 1985;17:117–32.")]. They produced polymers with a central, hollow core and tendrils that branched outward, one from another, in a precise, predictable manner, which Tomalia called dendrimers [[4](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR4 "Tomalia DA, Fréchet JMJ. Discovery of dendrimers and dendritic polymers: a brief historical perspective. J Polym Sci A Polym Chem. 2002;40:2719–28.")]. These two scientific groups contributed to the early history of dendrimers. Up to now, more than 100 dendritic structures have been reported, in which polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers, polypropyleneimine (PPI) dendrimers, as well as polyamide-, polyether-, polyester-, and phosphorus-based dendrimers are some of the most commonly recognized dendritic families [[2](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR2 "Buhleier E, Wehner W, Vögtle F. “Cascade”- and “nonskid-chain-like” syntheses of molecular cavity topologies. Synthesis. 1978;9:155–8."), [3](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR3 "Tomalia DA, Baker H, Dewald J, Hall M, Kallos G, Martin S, et al. A new class of polymers: starburst-dendritic macromolecules. Polym J. 1985;17:117–32."), [5](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR5 "Liko F, Hindré F, Fernandez-Megia E. Dendrimers as innovative radiopharmaceuticals in cancer radionanotherapy. Biomacromol. 2016;17:3103–14."),[6](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR6 "Mintzer MA, Grinstaff MW. Biomedical applications of dendrimers: a tutorial. Chem Soc Rev. 2011;40:173–90."),[7](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR7 "Rosen BM, Wilson CJ, Wilson DA, Peterca M, Imam MR, Percec V. Dendron-mediated self-assembly, disassembly, and self-organization of complex systems. Chem Rev. 2009;109:6275–540."),[8](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR8 "Wang D, Astruc D. Dendritic catalysis—basic concepts and recent trends. Coord Chem Rev. 2013;257:2317–34."),[9](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR9 "Esfand R, Tomalia DA. Poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) dendrimers: from biomimicry to drug delivery and biomedical applications. Drug Discov Today. 2001;6:427–36."),[10](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR10 "Idris AO, Mamba B, Feleni U. Poly (propylene imine) dendrimer: a potential nanomaterial for electrochemical application. Mater Chem Phys. 2020;244: 122641."),[11](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR11 "Caminade A-M, Majoral J-P. Nanomaterials based on phosphorus dendrimers. Acc Chem Res. 2004;37:341–8."),[12](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR12 "de Brabander van den Berg EMM, Meijer EW. Poly(propylene imine) dendrimers: large-scale synthesis by hetereogeneously catalyzed hydrogenations. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl. 1993;32:1308–11."),[13](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR13 "Robert G. Denkewalter, Jaroslav Kolc, Lukasavage WJ. Macromolecular highly branched homogenerous compound based on lysine units. United States Patent, 4,289,872, Sep.15, 1981."),[14](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR14 "Gillies ER, Fréchet JMJ. Designing macromolecules for therapeutic applications: polyester dendrimer poly(ethylene oxide) “Bow-Tie” hybrids with tunable molecular weight and architecture. J Am Chem Soc. 2002;124:14137–46."),[15](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR15 "Launay N, Caminade A-M, Lahana R, Majoral J-P. A general synthetic strategy for neutral phosphorus-containing dendrimers. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl. 1994;33:1589–92.")] (Table [1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Tab1)). Furthermore, thanks to the development of various synthetic strategies, including efficient orthogonal click chemistry and multicomponent reaction (MCR), many new dendrimers with the efficient synthetic process and structure diversity have emerged [[16](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR16 "Arseneault M, Wafer C, Morin JF. Recent advances in click chemistry applied to dendrimer synthesis. Molecules. 2015;20:9263–94."),[17](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR17 "Deng X-X, Du F-S, Li Z-C. Combination of orthogonal ABB and ABC multicomponent reactions toward efficient divergent synthesis of dendrimers with structural diversity. ACS Macro Lett. 2014;3:667–70."),[18](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR18 "Fan X, Hu Z, Wang G. Facile synthesis of polyester dendrimer via combining thio-bromo “Click” chemistry and ATNRC. J Polym Sci A Polym Chem. 2015;53:1762–8."),[19](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR19 "Jee J-A, Spagnuolo LA, Rudick JG. Convergent synthesis of dendrimers via the passerini three-component reaction. Org Lett. 2012;14:3292–5.")]. All the development has promoted the flourishing of dendrimers and their applications in chemistry, materials, and biological and medical science.

**Table 1 Several commonly recognized dendrimers**

[Full size table](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5/tables/1)

Dendrimer is different from traditional linear polymers by its mono-dispersity, high symmetricity, and surface polyvalency [[20](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR20 "Tomalia DA, Hedstrand DM, Ferritto MS. Comb-burst dendrimer topology: new macromolecular architecture derived from dendritic grafting. Macromolecules. 1991;24:1435–8."), [21](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR21 "Abbasi E, Aval SF, Akbarzadeh A, Milani M, Nasrabadi HT, Joo SW, et al. Dendrimers: synthesis, applications, and properties. Nanoscale Res Lett. 2014;9:247.")]. The repeated growth reactions during dendrimer synthesis lead to higher generation and degree of branching, eventually forming a three-dimensional spherical structure [[20](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR20 "Tomalia DA, Hedstrand DM, Ferritto MS. Comb-burst dendrimer topology: new macromolecular architecture derived from dendritic grafting. Macromolecules. 1991;24:1435–8.")]. The distinct synthetic process makes dendrimer possess a well-defined core–shell architecture and narrow polydispersity [[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR1 "Vögtle F, Richardt G, Werner N. Introduction. In: Vögtle F, Richardt G, Werner N, editors. Dendrimer chemistry: concepts, syntheses, properties, applications. Wiley: Academic; 2009. p. 1–24."), [9](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR9 "Esfand R, Tomalia DA. Poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) dendrimers: from biomimicry to drug delivery and biomedical applications. Drug Discov Today. 2001;6:427–36.")]. The size, surface charge, peripheral functional groups, and solubility of dendrimer could also be controlled by the synthetic process [[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR1 "Vögtle F, Richardt G, Werner N. Introduction. In: Vögtle F, Richardt G, Werner N, editors. Dendrimer chemistry: concepts, syntheses, properties, applications. Wiley: Academic; 2009. p. 1–24.")]. For instance, higher-generation dendrimers possess bigger size, larger interior cavity, and more terminal functional groups. In addition to the routine analytical methods, including nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), gel permeation chromatography (GPC), dynamic light scattering (DLS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), etc., characterization including electrophoretic and mass spectroscopy measurements have been developed to detect the mono-dispersity features and the effective charge of dendrimers [[22](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR22 "Shi X, Majoros IJ, Baker JR. Capillary electrophoresis of poly(amidoamine) dendrimers: from simple derivatives to complex multifunctional medical nanodevices. Mol Pharm. 2005;2:278–94."), [23](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR23 "Bosman AW, Janssen HM, Meijer EW. About dendrimers: structure, physical properties, and applications. Chem Rev. 1999;99:1665–88.")]. By using capillary electrophoresis, the purity, electrophoretic mobility, and molecular charge distribution of various surface-charged PAMAM dendrimer nanoparticles can be assessed [[22](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR22 "Shi X, Majoros IJ, Baker JR. Capillary electrophoresis of poly(amidoamine) dendrimers: from simple derivatives to complex multifunctional medical nanodevices. Mol Pharm. 2005;2:278–94.")]. The nanoscopic characteristics of individual PAMAM dendrimers from generations 5 to 10 (G5 to G10) were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) reported by Jackson and coworkers in 1998 [[24](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR24 "Jackson CL, Chanzy HD, Booy FP, Drake BJ, Tomalia DA, Bauer BJ, Amis EJ. Visualization of dendrimer molecules by transmission electron microscopy (TEM): staining methods and cryo-TEM of vitrified solutions. Macromolecules. 1998;31:6259–65.")]. The mean diameters increase from 4.3 nm for PAMAM G5 to 14.7 nm for PAMAM G10. Besides the nanoscopic feature, the multivalency of dendrimers makes them amenable to further modification or conjugation with chemical species such as fluorophores, targeting ligands, drugs, and genes (Fig.[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1**

[![Image 5: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs13036-022-00298-5/MediaObjects/13036_2022_298_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5/figures/1)

Structure diversity, adaptability and flexible cargo loading capacity of dendrimers. G1, G2, and G3 represent generation 1, 2, and 3, respectively

[Full size image](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5/figures/1)

### Dendrimers as drug delivery vehicles

Dendrimers have emerged as an important group of nanostructured carriers for the development of nanomedicine to treat various diseases. Because of structural diversity and adaptability, dendrimers have been used to deliver drugs and genes in many different ways (Fig.[1](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Fig1)). For instance, dendrimers with a hydrophobic core and a hydrophilic periphery may behave like unimolecular micelles, and they have been utilized to solubilize hydrophobic drugs by entrapping them in the intramolecular cavity [[25](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR25 "Menjoge AR, Kannan RM, Tomalia DA. Dendrimer-based drug and imaging conjugates: design considerations for nanomedical applications. Drug Discov Today. 2010;15:171–85."), [26](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR26 "Rao BN, Viswanath V, Reddy KR, Fathima SR, Surekha P, Bhuvaneswari S. Dendrimers–structure, synthesis, encapsulation, characterization and application. J Global Trends Pharm Sci. 2015;6:2860–6.")]. Cationic dendrimers have been extensively applied as non-viral gene carriers [[27](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR27 "Parekh HS. The advance of dendrimers–a versatile targeting platform for gene/drug delivery. Curr Pharm Design. 2007;13:2837–50.")]. Dendrimer surface groups can be conjugated with drugs and other functional moieties (Fig.[2](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Fig2)a) [[28](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR28 "Kurtoglu YE, Mishra MK, Kannan S, Kannan RM. Drug release characteristics of PAMAM dendrimer–drug conjugates with different linkers. Int J Pharm. 2010;384:189–94."), [29](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR29 "Gupta V, Nayak S. Dendrimers: A review on synthetic approaches. J Appl Pharm Sci. 2015;5:117–22.")]. Conjugating dendrimers with polymers such as polyethylene glycol (PEG), polysaccharide, and polypeptide mainly enhances stability and solubility of the therapeutics to be delivered [[30](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR30 "Liu J, Gray WD, Davis ME, Luo Y. Peptide- and saccharide-conjugated dendrimers for targeted drug delivery: a concise review. Interface Focus. 2012;2:307–24.")]. PEGylation of dendrimers is a common process through which PEG chains are conjugated to dendrimers, forming a unimolecular micelle [[31](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR31 "Yang H, Morris JJ, Lopina ST. Polyethylene glycol–polyamidoamine dendritic micelle as solubility enhancer and the effect of the length of polyethylene glycol arms on the solubility of pyrene in water. J Colloid Interface Sci. 2004;273:148–54.")]. Dendrimer-polysaccharide conjugates are usually adopted to endow the nanomaterials with attractive binding properties and improved compatibility [[30](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR30 "Liu J, Gray WD, Davis ME, Luo Y. Peptide- and saccharide-conjugated dendrimers for targeted drug delivery: a concise review. Interface Focus. 2012;2:307–24."), [32](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR32 "Pooresmaeil M, Namazi H. Advances in development of the dendrimers having natural saccharides in their structure for efficient and controlled drug delivery applications. Eur Polym J. 2021;148: 110356."), [33](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR33 "Agrawal P, Gupta U, Jain NK. Glycoconjugated peptide dendrimers-based nanoparticulate system for the delivery of chloroquine phosphate. Biomaterials. 2007;28:3349–59.")]. Polysaccharides, for instance chitosan, hyaluronic acid, cyclodextrin, and dextran have been broadly conjugated to dendrimers [[34](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR34 "Sharma AK, Gupta L, Sahu H, Qayum A, Singh SK, Nakhate KT, et al. Chitosan engineered PAMAM dendrimers as nanoconstructs for the enhanced anti-cancer potential and improved in vivo brain pharmacokinetics of temozolomide. Pharm Res. 2018;35:9."),[35](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR35 "Han M, Huang-Fu M-Y, Guo W-W, Guo N-N, Chen J, Liu H-N, et al. MMP-2-Sensitive HA end-conjugated poly(amidoamine) dendrimers via click reaction to enhance drug penetration into solid tumor. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces. 2017;9:42459–70."),[36](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR36 "Arima H, Motoyama K, Higashi T. Sugar-appended polyamidoamine dendrimer conjugates with cyclodextrins as cell-specific non-viral vectors. Adv Drug Deliv Rev. 2013;65:1204–14."),[37](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR37 "Agarwal A, Gupta U, Asthana A, Jain NK. Dextran conjugated dendritic nanoconstructs as potential vectors for anti-cancer agent. Biomaterials. 2009;30:3588–96.")]. A hyaluronic acid conjugated PAMAM dendrimer showed enhanced tumor penetration property due to the strong affinity of hyaluronic acid to CD44 receptors, which are overexpressed on tumor cells and cancer stem cells [[35](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR35 "Han M, Huang-Fu M-Y, Guo W-W, Guo N-N, Chen J, Liu H-N, et al. MMP-2-Sensitive HA end-conjugated poly(amidoamine) dendrimers via click reaction to enhance drug penetration into solid tumor. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces. 2017;9:42459–70.")]. He et al. reported a mannose conjugated PAMAM dendrimer for the targeted delivery of liver-x-receptor (LXR) ligands to macrophages as mannose can bind specifically to mannose reporter expressed on macrophage surface [[38](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR38 "He H, Yuan Q, Bie J, Wallace RL, Yannie PJ, Wang J, et al. Development of mannose functionalized dendrimeric nanoparticles for targeted delivery to macrophages: use of this platform to modulate atherosclerosis. Transl Res. 2018;193:13–30.")].

**Fig. 2**

[![Image 6: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs13036-022-00298-5/MediaObjects/13036_2022_298_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5/figures/2)

Ways to utilize dendrimers to deliver drugs and genes: **a** dendrimer conjugates and cleavable linkers used to make dendrimer conjugates, **b** three drug/dendrimer encapsulation models, **c** dendrimer/gene complexation, and how cationic dendrimer helps enhance transfection efficiency

[Full size image](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5/figures/2)

#### Dendrimer-drug conjugates

Dendrimer-drug conjugates could reduce systemic effects and increase efficacy at the targeted site compared with free drugs [[25](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR25 "Menjoge AR, Kannan RM, Tomalia DA. Dendrimer-based drug and imaging conjugates: design considerations for nanomedical applications. Drug Discov Today. 2010;15:171–85."), [39](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR39 "Kolhe P, Khandare J, Pillai O, Kannan S, Lieh-Lai M, Kannan RM. Preparation, cellular transport, and activity of polyamidoamine-based dendritic nanodevices with a high drug payload. Biomaterials. 2006;27:660–9.")]. It is reported that the half-life of drugs can be increased by conjugating with dendrimers. For instance, the half-life of methotrexate is increased to 24 h from 24 min when conjugated with PAMAM dendrimer [[29](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR29 "Gupta V, Nayak S. Dendrimers: A review on synthetic approaches. J Appl Pharm Sci. 2015;5:117–22.")]. The extended circulating half-life can thus enhance the drug efficacy and lower the frequency of drug administration, improving patient compliance. Solubility of drugs is enhanced when conjugated with dendrimers. For example, paclitaxel’s water solubility is enhanced by 9000-fold when conjugated with PAMAM dendrimer [[29](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR29 "Gupta V, Nayak S. Dendrimers: A review on synthetic approaches. J Appl Pharm Sci. 2015;5:117–22.")]. We previously reported a dendrimer-drug conjugate of DenTimol and studied its therapeutic efficacy for glaucoma treatment [[40](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR40 "Lancina MG, Wang J, Williamson GS, Yang H. DenTimol as a dendrimeric timolol analogue for glaucoma therapy: synthesis and preliminary efficacy and safety assessment. Mol Pharm. 2018;15:2883–9.")]. An antiglaucoma drug precursor, (S)-4-[4-(oxiranylmethoxy)-1,2,5-thiadiazol-3-yl]morpholine (OTM), was conjugated to PAMAM dendrimer surface through a PEG spacer. DenTimol is efficient at crossing the cornea due to the good mucoadhesive property of dendrimer, and about 8% of the dendrimer-drug permeated through the cornea in 4 h. DenTimol demonstrated a stronger intraocular pressure (IOP) lowering effect than timolol maleate in normotensive adult Brown Norway male rats. A single dose of DenTimol (10 μL of 0.5% w/v timolol) resulted in an IOP reduction by an average of 7.3 mmHg in less than 0.5 h, which was significantly stronger than timolol PBS eye drops.

According to the United Food and Drug Association (FDA), dendrimer-drug conjugates may be classified into new drugs or combinational devices [[41](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR41 "Lancina MG, Yang H. Dendrimers for ocular drug delivery. Can J Chem. 2017;95:897–902.")]. If drugs can be cleaved from dendrimer-drug conjugates and maintain their initial structure, this bureaucratic regulation issue could be avoided. Therefore, it is important to examine the release of drugs from dendrimer-drug conjugates (Fig.[2](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#Fig2)a) [[28](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR28 "Kurtoglu YE, Mishra MK, Kannan S, Kannan RM. Drug release characteristics of PAMAM dendrimer–drug conjugates with different linkers. Int J Pharm. 2010;384:189–94.")]. The most facile way is to conjugate drug to dendrimer via a cleavable or stimuli-liable bond. Disulfide and thioketal linkers can be cleaved by glutathione and reactive oxygen species (ROS) in tumor cells, respectively. Therefore, they have been broadly used in designing cleavable dendrimer-drug conjugates [[42](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR42 "Wang G, Zhou Z, Zhao Z, Li Q, Wu Y, Yan S, et al. Enzyme-triggered transcytosis of dendrimer–drug conjugate for deep penetration into pancreatic tumors. ACS Nano. 2020;14:4890–904."),[43](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR43 "Jiang W, Luo X, Wei L, Yuan S, Cai J, Jiang X, et al. The sustainability of energy conversion inhibition for tumor ferroptosis therapy and chemotherapy. Small. 2021;17:2102695."),[44](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR44 "Liu H, Wang H, Yang W, Cheng Y. Disulfide cross-linked low generation dendrimers with high gene transfection efficacy, low cytotoxicity, and low cost. J Am Chem Soc. 2012;134:17680–7."),[45](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR45 "Shen Y, Zhou Z, Sui M, Tang J, Xu P, Kirk EAV, et al. Charge-reversal polyamidoamine dendrimer for cascade nuclear drug delivery. Nanomedicine. 2010;5:1205–17.")]. pH-responsible linkers, especially acid-liable bonds (e.g., acetal bond), are also used to make dendrimer-drug conjugates for cancer therapy due to the acidic tumor microenvironment [[46](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR46 "Pang X, Jiang Y, Xiao Q, Leung AW, Hua H, Xu C. pH-Responsive polymer–drug conjugates: design and progress. J Control Release. 2016;222:116–29.")]. We have constructed a dendrimer-camptothecin-based hydrogel drug delivery system [[47](https://jbioleng.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13036-022-00298-5#ref-CR47 "Wang J, He H, Cooper RC, Gui Q, Yang H. Drug-conjugated dendrimer hydrogel enables sustained drug release via a self-cleaving mechanism. Mol Pharm. 2019;16:1874–80.")]. In this novel drug delivery system, a pH-controlled self-cleaving release of camptothecin (CPT) is realized via the ammonolysis of ester bonds between the dendrimer and CPT. The controlled self-cleaving release mechanism significantly prolonged CPT release and thus enhanced tumor inhibition.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Dendrimers are unique highly branched polymers featuring a central core with multiple functional groups, providing a three-dimensional structure ideal for encapsulating drugs and genes.
Their modular design allows for precise customization of size, charge, and surface functional groups, enabling targeted drug delivery tailored to specific therapeutic needs.
Dendrimers can be engineered to release therapeutic agents in response to specific stimuli (e.g., pH, enzymes), allowing for controlled and sustained drug release at targeted sites.
They are versatile carriers, applicable in various therapeutic areas, including cancer, gene therapy, and anti-inflammatory treatments, enhancing drug solubility and bioavailability.
